data.title: Fetterman labels Mamdani 'clown' over Netanyahu arrest threat
data.description:
Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) on Wednesday called New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, a “clown” for floating the idea of arresting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for war crimes allegations.

“You are a mayor, dude,” Fetterman said about Mamdani in an interview with Fox News’s Sean Hannity. “You’re a mayor. You can fix a pothole. You can take out the trash. But you can’t order people to arrest anybody any more than I could, or, you know, I could have him arrested if he goes to Pennsylvania.

“It’s a joke,” Fetterman added, “and he’s a clown.”

The Pennsylvania Democrat, who has become an increasingly strong supporter of Israel in the years since the start of the country’s response to the Oct. 7, 2023, Hamas attack, said Mamdani should “sit down,” accusing him of pandering “to the fringe.”

“And I know you’re angry at Netanyahu because he killed a lot of people you admire, whether –– like that Sinwar, or Nasrallah or Khamenei in Iran,” Fetterman said, referring to former Hamas leader in the Gaza Strip Yahya Sinwar, former Hezbollah secretary-general Hassan Nasrallah and former Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.

Fetterman made similar remarks on Fox News on Sunday, telling host Maria Bartiromo that Mamdani was a “tough guy.”

“That’s really not your purview,” he said about the mayor. “You and I know that. Sit down.”

Mamdani said during the mayoral campaign last year that he would have Netanyahu arrested, following the International Criminal Court (ICC) issuing a warrant for Netanyahu’s arrest in 2024 for alleged war crimes and crimes against humanity in Palestine, including “starvation as a method of warfare” and “other inhumane acts” against the Palestinian population in Gaza.

With Netanyahu scheduled to come to New York City for the annual United Nations General Assembly in September, Mamdani’s campaign promise has come under scrutiny.

He recently told The New York Times that he would follow the city’s laws. However, on Tuesday, he said the city does “not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant.”

“And I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant,” Mamdani said in a video posted on the social platform X.

Israel and the U.S. are not subject to the rulings of the ICC, as both countries have never ratified the Rome Statute — the treaty that created the ICC — and do not recognize the court’s jurisdiction over its citizens, service members, or government officials. 

Netanyahu, with an Israeli Knesset election looming in October, has rejected the ICC’s arrest warrant and denied wrongdoing. His office, in response to Mamdani’s threat to arrest the prime minister, called the ICC a “kangaroo court.”

“Mr. Mamdani should focus on fixing the damage his policies have caused New York,” Netanyahu’s office said in a statement last week. “… Mamdani appears interested in diverting public attention from his follies and attacking the leader of the Jewish state and the only democracy in the Middle East.”

data.title: Cassidy cites autism website change in support of CDC nominee
data.description:
Presented by PhRMA
{beacon}

Healthcare

Health Care

  PRESENTED BY 

The Big Story 

Cassidy cites autism website change in support of CDC nominee  

Dr. Erica Schwartz, President Trump’s pick to lead the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), picked up a key endorsement from Senate Health Committee Chairman Bill Cassidy (R-La.) on Thursday. 

Natasha Kaiser

During her confirmation hearing last week, Cassidy grew frustrated when Schwartz dodged questions about whether she could protect the agency from political meddling and stand up to Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. if he asked her to take action that was not supported by scientific evidence.  

 

But Cassidy, who lost a primary election to a challenger backed by President Trump, said he now trusts that Schwartz will make the right decisions.  

 

“I’m confident that she knows what she is doing and will stand against those who do not,” Cassidy said. 

 

Cassidy added that “as a sign of good faith,” the HHS had modified the CDC’s website on vaccines and autism. 

 

Last year Kennedy ordered the page to contradict the scientifically settled idea that vaccines do not cause autism.  

 

“There’s more to do to repair the damage that has been done to the U.S. public health response, but this is a start,” Cassidy said.  

  

But the page’s central claim hasn’t changed; it still says that studies have not disproven a link between vaccines and autism, and that “studies supporting a link have been ignored by health authorities.”  

 

What has changed is a footnote beneath the headline “Vaccines do not cause autism” no longer references Cassidy.  

 

The earlier version pointedly said the headline had not been removed because of an agreement with the “chair of the Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee.”  

 

The update says scientists have not identified the root causes of autism and that the agency will update the page as new evidence emerges.

 

Welcome to The Hill’s Healthcare newsletter, we’re Nathaniel Weixel and Joseph Choi — every week we follow the latest moves on how Washington impacts your health.

 Full Story
 

Democrats urge appeals court not to overturn mifepristone regulations

The entire congressional Democratic caucus filed an amicus brief urging a federal appeals court to retain the Food and Drug Administration’s current rules for prescribing abortion pills.  Forty-seven Democratic senators and 212 House members asked the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 5th Circuit not to reinstate an in-person requirement to obtain mifepristone, which has not been enforced in five years.   The brief was …

 Full Story
 

Another cyclospora outbreak not linked to lettuce under investigation: FDA

(NEXSTAR) – A new outbreak of cyclospora, the parasite capable of giving you “explosive” diarrhea, that has not been linked to the previously recalled lettuce is now under investigation by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. The latest outbreak, with 72 cases connected to it, was added to the FDA’s list of ongoing investigations on Wednesday.

 Full Story
 
 
 

CONTENT FROM OUR SPONSOR

When America leads in medicine development,
every American benefits.

Americans get new medicines 3 years faster than other high-income countries thanks to our biopharmaceutical leadership. If Washington copies other countries’ price control policies, we risk America’s access to cures. Learn more

 
 

In Other News 

Branch out with a different read from The Hill:

Trump to impose new double-digit tariffs on imports from 60 countries

President Trump will impose double-digit tariffs on imports from 60 countries over forced labor violations, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced Thursday. The action will cover 99.4 percent of U.S. imports and apply to Washington’s top trading partners, according to a fact sheet released by his office.

Full Story

data.title: Massachusetts House passes bill loosening late-term abortion restrictions
data.description:
Massachusetts state House lawmakers on Wednesday passed a bill loosening restrictions on late-term abortion access.

The bill, H.5595, passed 119-33 and will go to the state Senate, state House Speaker Ronald Mariano (D) said in a statement. If it passes the Senate, it will allow an abortion after 24 weeks, if approved by a doctor.

State law allows abortions to be performed on pregnant people at or after 24 weeks to protect the person’s life, their physical or mental health, or if there is a fatal or severe fetal condition with the child.

This restriction has led to many patients leaving the state for access. State Rep. Christine Barber (D) told the Boston Globe that this prompted the bill’s creation.

“Patients have to leave to other states, leave their support network, and pay out of pocket,” Barber told the Globe. “You should be able to get care that your doctors are recommending here in Massachusetts.”

Barber referred to one case of a woman, Kate Dineen, who traveled 500 miles with her husband to a Maryland clinic in 2022 while 33 weeks pregnant. Her baby suffered a stroke in utero. She paid about $10,000 out of pocket for the procedure, the outlet reported.

“Massachusetts has one of the best health care systems in the country, and patients should be able to rely on their providers to deliver medically appropriate care without restrictions that can fail to account for the complexity of individual medical circumstances,” state Rep. Lindsay Sabadosa (D), the bill’s co-sponsor, said in a statement.

The anti-abortion group Massachusetts Citizens for Life slammed the bill’s passage but said its sponsors had “good intentions.” They pointed to the bill permitting one doctor “to subjectively decide that any unborn infant can be killed in or outside of a hospital,” the group’s president, Myrna Maloney Flynn, said in a statement

“Women experiencing unimaginable, heart wrenching pregnancy complications absolutely deserve all the support they need, but that support is not what this bill provides,” Flynn said. “Instead, this bill ensures increased abortion industry profits and taxpayer-funded late-term procedures.”

The bill received support from several major health organizations in the Bay State, including the Massachusetts Health and Hospital Association, Mass General Brigham, UMass Memorial Health, Tufts Medicine and Beth Israel Lahey Health.

The associations and health systems previously called on Mariano to pass the bill, writing in a letter that it would “empower physicians to use their professional judgment and clinical expertise to make decisions in the best interest of their patients.”

The bill will now go to the Senate for consideration and must pass before the legislative session ends on July 31. Massachusetts Gov. Maura Healey (D), who supports reproductive health and abortion access, has not indicated if she will sign the bill.

data.title: Fauci to face Rand Paul in Senate hearing next week
data.description:
Dr. Anthony Fauci will testify next Wednesday in front of the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee after being subpoenaed, chairman Rand Paul (R-Ky.) said Thursday.  

Paul has been laser-focused on Fauci, the nation’s former top infectious diseases doctor, for allegedly covering up the U.S.’s involvement in risky viral research in a Chinese lab that the Kentucky senator says created the virus that caused the COVID-19 pandemic.  

 “I expect he will not tell the truth,” Paul said in a Fox Business interview Thursday. “I don’t expect him to be honest, he hasn’t been honest in the last six years.”  

The two men clashed repeatedly during heated Senate hearings when Fauci was leading the country’s pandemic response during the Biden administration.  

Fauci retired from government service in 2022 and has repeatedly denied accusations from Paul and other Republicans. 

Paul has claimed without hard evidence that the virus was manufactured in a Chinese lab and somehow escaped, and that Fauci and other top officials conspired to convince the public that the “lab leak” idea was a preposterous conspiracy theory. He blames Fauci for the tens of millions of COVID-19 deaths worldwide.   

President Joe Biden preemptively pardoned Fauci and other top officials before Biden left office, over concerns they would be targeted by the Trump administration. 

Paul has also called for Fauci to be indicted for what the senator said was lying to Congress under oath about gain-of-function research.  

Infectious disease experts have said the work the National Institutes of Health was funding could not have caused the pandemic.

data.title: Patel returns looted artifacts to Indonesian president
data.description:
A collection of cultural artifacts from Indonesia’s Papua region that were looted and illegally taken to the U.S. have been returned to Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto, Indonesian officials confirmed to reporters.

The handover of the artifacts took place Wednesday at Prabowo’s residence in the Indonesian capital Jakarta during a meeting with FBI Director Kash Patel.

Indonesia’s Culture Minister Fadli Zon told reporters after the meeting that Patel personally handed the artifacts to Prabowo as part of an ongoing effort to recover and return cultural artifacts.

“Besides our official discussions, Patel also brought Indonesian cultural artifacts as part of cooperation between the Indonesian government, through the Culture Ministry, and the FBI to return cultural objects that had been illegally smuggled into the United States,” Zon said.

The recovered collection originating from Papua included stone ax heads and ceremonial objects traced to the Dani, Asmat and Sentani tribes.

“These are very valuable objects because this repatriation process is part of our effort to restore our cultural sovereignty,” Zon said. 

He added that the artifacts will later be displayed at Indonesia’s National Museum, where the public will be able to view and study Indonesia’s cultural heritage.

Zon said the FBI had previously assisted Indonesia with recovering stolen cultural objects and said he hoped that cooperation will continue.

The handoff follows a series of recent efforts by the U.S. to return looted Southeast Asian antiquities that were illegally smuggled from the region. 

Earlier this month, two eighth-century bronze Buddhist sculptures depicting bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara were also returned after being stolen from an Indonesian archaeological site and trafficked through an American antiquities dealer.

data.title: Ratepayer bill gains steam amid data center backlash
data.description:
{beacon}

Technology

Technology

 

The Big Story 

Ratepayer bill gains steam amid data center backlash

A bill that seeks to mitigate data centers’ impacts on Americans’ electric bills is gaining steam in the House, as lawmakers face pressure to act in the face of sharp backlash to the sprawling AI infrastructure.

© AP Photo/Jenny Kane

The bipartisan Ratepayer Protection Act would require states to “consider” standards that put the costs on tech companies, rather than individuals.

 

Its supporters say it could help alleviate potential price spikes caused by power-hungry data centers, but critics say it doesn’t go far enough to solve the problem.

 

The legislation advanced out of the House Energy and Commerce Committee on Tuesday in a 52-0 vote, a strong display of bipartisan support and a reflection of how the backlash to the data center buildout has crossed party lines.

 

The measure, which aims to codify a White House pledge signed by tech giants earlier this year, forces state regulators to hold a hearing on a standard that would ensure that large data centers pay for the cost of new power or power lines stemming from their electricity use. It does not force them to actually adopt such standards.

 

“[I]t has become clear that across the entire industry there is one entity uniquely positioned to stand up for families and communities who are the ones paying electricity bills-and that is this Committee, and our colleagues in Congress,” House Energy and Commerce Chair Brett Guthrie (R-Ky.) said in a statement.

 

The Ratepayer Protection Act also recently got a companion in the Senate, bolstering its chances of becoming law.

 

Sen. Jon Husted (R-Ohio) introduced the measure in the upper chamber last week.

 

A spokesperson for the senator said in a statement that he is “encouraged by the bipartisan support and committee passage in the House” and will continue working with the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee to get the bill “across the finish line in the Senate.”

 

Read more in a full report at TheHill.com.

 

Welcome to The Hill’s Technology newsletter, we’re Julia Shapero and Miranda Nazzaro — tracking the latest moves from Capitol Hill to Silicon Valley.

Thirty-six percent of respondents to the Pew Research Center poll think China is more advanced on AI than the U.S., while just 12 percent believe it is the other way around. One-third of respondents were …

Trump defends data centers as he expands pledge to make them ‘pay their own way’

President Trump on Thursday expanded his pledge that aims to have data centers “pay their own way” for the electricity they use, but he took the opportunity to defend data centers at the same time. Trump announced that entities ranging from utilities to state governments are joining the pledge, which was already signed by major tech companies. The pledge, which is voluntary, says that data centers should either …

China says AI development comes from ‘greater self-reliance and strength’ amid stolen tech claims

China defended the country’s development of artificial intelligence, stating it comes from “greater self-reliance and strength” after a top White House official accused a Chinese startup of improperly using Anthropic’s latest model and accessing restricted Nvidia chips. Lin Jian, China’s Foreign Ministry spokesperson, told reporters on Wednesday the country “opposes politicizing and instrumentalizing …

 Full Story
 

Google hit with $1B fine in Europe

The European Union fined Google about $1 billion on Thursday for violating its digital competition law, finding the company improperly favored its own products in Google Search and limited developers from directing users to third-party app stores. The EU’s executive arm, the European Commission, hit the company with a 460 million euro fine and a 430 million euro fine over the two violations of the Digital Markets Act …

 Full Story

Michael Kratsios, the director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy, said the administration has information that Moonshot AI distilled Anthropic’s Fable to produce Kimi K3. Distillation refers to a technique used …

data.title: Smith backs El-Sayed in Michigan Senate race
data.description:
Sen. Tina Smith (D-Minn.) is backing Abdul El-Sayed in the Michigan Senate race, the Minnesota Democrat said Thursday.

“Abdul is a fighter, and that’s why I’m endorsing him to be the next United States Senator for Michigan,” Smith wrote in a post on the social platform X Thursday morning.

Smith’s post also featured a video, where she stated that El-Sayed is best positioned to win the Michigan race “because he is the one who has the capacity to build the grassroots movement that is really going to power Democrats to victory in November.”

In his own post on X Thursday, El-Sayed said Smith “has been a fighter for working people in the US Senate.”

“I hope to build on her leadership on women’s health and reproductive rights and access which has paved the way for the future I want for my daughters and every American,” he added.

El-Sayed, a progressive, will face off against Rep. Haley Stevens (D-Mich.), a moderate, for Sen. Gary Peters’s (D-Mich.) seat in the Democratic primary in August.

Smith joins fellow Sens. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.), Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) and Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) in support for El-Sayed’s bid. 

Those who support Stevens’s bid include Peters, former Michigan Sen. Debbie Stabenow (D), Democratic Sens. Chris Coons (Del.) and Catherine Cortez Masto (Nev.) and former Michigan Gov. Jennifer Granholm (D).

In a recent poll, Stevens led El-Sayed in the primary by about 7 points, with 48.2 percent support to El-Sayed’s 41.4 percent.

data.title: Thune says Iran sanctions to be added to Russia sanctions bill
data.description:
Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) told reporters Thursday he’s expecting to add provisions to penalize Iran to a Russia sanctions bill under consideration in the upper chamber. 

President Trump said on social media over the weekend that he wanted Iran sanctions added to the bipartisan Russia-related legislation.

“I think everybody’s fine with it because we do it anyway, Iran sanctions is something we’ve had in place the last 30 years,” Thune said. “I’ve been having conversations with Democrats and folks on our side, and I think we’re I think we’re homing in on it.”

The Russia bill has been in the works for years, but has picked up steam and support from the White House since the death of the late Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) earlier this month. Graham was a chief driver of the bill, which would impose mandatory sanctions on Russia and its allies, possibly including penalties on buyers of Russian energy in the form of tariffs. Now, Iran and its ally in Lebanon, Hezbollah, could be included under the sanctions umbrella. 

Thune said he is seeking an agreement with Democrats to shorten the time the bill can be considered on the Senate floor, allowing it a vote in the full Senate as soon as possible. Senators are scheduled to leave town for a month on August 7.

“I’m hoping we can get it teed up, and then if we can get some sort of limited time agreement to help expedite it on the floor, that would help too,” Thune added.

data.title: Trump administration drops demand that Penn hand over list of Jewish employees
data.description:
The Trump administration has backed away from efforts to subpoena a list of Jewish employees at the University of Pennsylvania, one of several schools it has accused of failing to stop antisemitism, according to court documents filed Thursday.

The documents, filed with the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit, show the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) has made an agreement with the university to “not take any further measures to enforce the subpoena seeking identification and personal contact information of employees of the University of Pennsylvania.”

In exchange, Penn agreed to dismiss its appeal of the request, ending more than a year of litigation over the move.

“With the support of many members of the community who have stood with Penn throughout, the University has been able to protect its employees’ rights and none of the subpoenaed personal contact information was disclosed,” a university spokesperson said in a statement. “Penn remains committed to fighting antisemitism and all other forms of discrimination, to ensure everyone it serves can learn, work and thrive.”

An EEOC spokesperson declined to comment beyond the court filings.

The EEOC sought information on employees affiliated with Jewish organizations, including the university’s Jewish Studies program, to identify potential witnesses or victims of antisemitism occurring on campus. 

Efforts to subpoena the information were part of a slate of investigations into allegations of antisemitism at Penn following Hamas’s Oct. 7, 2023 attack on Israel.

Penn objected to parts of the subpoena, arguing the requests threatened the privacy and safety of Jewish employees, along with raising constitutional concerns. The EEOC sued the university in November to enforce the subpoena.

“The E.E.O.C. insists that Penn produce this information without the consent — and indeed, over the objections — of the employees impacted while entirely disregarding the frightening and well-documented history of governmental entities that undertook efforts to identify and assemble information regarding persons of Jewish ancestry,” the university said in a court filing in January challenging the subpoena.

Under Thursday’s agreement, the EEOC also agreed not to seek enforcement of the district’s court order or otherwise pursue the subpoenaed information from Penn amid pending administrative investigations.

data.title: Almost 2 in 3 feel dissatisfied or upset with Trump ballroom, Reflecting Pool, arch projects: Survey
data.description:
A majority of Americans don’t approve of President Trump’s renovation efforts in the U.S.’s capital city, according to a new survey. 

The president has undertaken a series of controversial beautification projects around Washington, D.C., including updating the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, replacing the East Wing of the White House with a multi-million-dollar ballroom and constructing a massive replica of Paris’ Arc de Triomphe near the National Mall. 

A new Washington Post-Ipsos poll asked respondents how they felt about this trio of projects. Nearly 2 in 3 respondents — or 65 percent of those surveyed — reacted negatively, with 27 percent indicating dissatisfaction and 38 percent saying they were “upset” by these efforts. 

Among those with positive reactions, 12 percent said they were “enthusiastic” and 19 percent said they were “satisfied” with these projects.

The poll found a partisan split on this issue. Nearly 7 in 10 of Republican respondents expressed a positive view of these renovations, while about 9 in 10 of the survey’s Democratic respondents expressed a negative view. 

Additionally, the poll asked respondents about Trump’s motivations for these projects. Twenty-six percent said they believed he was committed to making the capital city “more beautiful,” while 51 percent said they thought the president had a “different goal.”

When the latter group was asked what they believed this alternate objective was, 42 percent said to “glorify himself” and 22 percent said to “leave his mark” on D.C.

The survey’s researchers noted that these results do not reveal discrepancies between perceptions of individual projects but instead group all three together. Previous polling has indicated that the public is more widely opposed to the ballroom project than the arch or Reflecting Pool. 

The proposed ballroom has raised concerns over its cost to taxpayers after the project’s price tag was raised from an initial $400 million to a reported $600 million earlier this year. Trump has defended the multi-million-dollar project, saying it would be covered entirely by private donations. 

However, records reported by the Post in June showed that only $293 million of the total cost would be covered by “private sources,” while the rest would come from taxpayer-funded department budgets. 

The Reflecting Pool project has also faced a wave of criticism over the peeling of its newly-installed “American flag blue” base and an algae outbreak shortly after the renovation’s completion. Crews drained the pool again after Independence Day to address these issues. 

The Washington Post-Ipsos poll surveyed over 2,600 U.S. adults from July 8-13 with a margin of error of approximately 1.9 percentage points.
